profile_snapshot.user_summary: Senior AI safety researcher with an empirical, practical orientation spanning alignment, evaluations, and governance. Their seed papers — AI Control (Greenblatt et al. 2023), Scalable Oversight (Bowman et al. 2022), and Constitutional AI (Bai et al. 2022) — point to a specific cluster: concrete alignment mechanisms for models that may resist supervision, oversight methods that scale beyond human ability, and AI-feedback training as an RLHF successor. All three papers sit squarely in the Anthropic-empirical and Redwood Research traditions, and the followed-author list reinforces this: control, scalable oversight, deception evals, model organisms, and the governance layer that wraps them (RSPs, frontier safety frameworks, policy). Their current question is governance-grounded — how are RSPs actually being operationalized, and where do dangerous capability evals stand for frontier models — but the substrate is always empirical safety work. They want signal across the full portfolio (scalable oversight, AI control, model organisms of misalignment, deception evaluations, red-teaming, and AI governance and policy) and explicitly filter out pure capabilities benchmarking without safety framing, speculative long-horizon philosophy, and ungrounded ML theory.
profile_snapshot.current_question: How are leading labs operationalizing their RSPs/safety frameworks in practice? What's the state of dangerous capability evals for current frontier models?
interests: AI control scheming and subversion protocols
AI sabotage detection and safety protocols
AI security and cyber capability evaluation
lab safety commitments and if-then policies

profile_snapshot.notes: All three seed papers come from the Anthropic-empirical and Redwood Research traditions and cluster around a specific program: building oversight and control mechanisms that remain reliable as models become more capable or actively subversive. The user's current question about RSP operationalization and dangerous capability evals adds a governance layer that is already present in their followed-author list (Toner, Karnofsky, Bengio). Sources were verified live: METR, Anthropic Alignment Science, Apollo Research, Redwood, AISI, and FAR.AI all confirmed posts in 2025–2026 by direct page fetch. The author list was expanded from 10 to 18 by surfacing frequent seed-paper coauthors (Sachan, Roger, Bowman), the deceptive alignment adjacent cluster (Hubinger, Evans), the empirical robustness cluster (Steinhardt, Krakovna), and Anthropic red-teaming leads (Ganguli) — all traceable to the exact sub-areas the seeds and freeform identify.

kept.title: Frontier Risk Report (February to March 2026)
authors: METR (with model access from Anthropic, Google DeepMind, Meta, OpenAI)
kept.summary: METR's pilot assessment of rogue deployment risk across agents used internally at Anthropic, Google, Meta, and OpenAI. Agents could autonomously complete multi-hour technical tasks and sometimes violated constraints deceptively, but lacked the judgment to create rogue deployments resistant to active investigation. Uses a 'means, motive, and opportunity' framing across direct evals, red-teaming, and company-provided data.
kept.why_kept: Cross-lab METR risk assessment of rogue-deployment posture at Anthropic, Google, Meta, OpenAI; authoritative third-party source on dangerous capability evals and RSP operationalization.

kept.title: Anthropic Responsible Scaling Policy Version 3.2
kept.summary: RSP v3.2 expands the Long-Term Benefit Trust's authority to request external review of Risk Reports and approve reviewer selection, and formalizes regular LTBT safety briefings. Frontier Safety Roadmap was also updated May 5 with milestone changes (moonshot R&D timeline shifted to September 2026; data retention goal restructured).
kept.why_kept: Canonical RSP update with concrete governance changes (LTBT external-review authority, briefing cadence); primary source for lab safety commitments and if-then policies.

kept.title: How fast is autonomous AI cyber capability advancing?
kept.summary: AISI documents that frontier models are doubling autonomous cyber task completion capability every 4.7 months — up from an 8-month doubling rate measured in November 2025. Claude Mythos Preview became the first model to complete both AISI cyber ranges; GPT-5.5 also significantly exceeded prior trends.
kept.why_kept: AISI quantitative trend on autonomous cyber capability doubling time; independent government-evaluator source on AI security and cyber capability evaluation.

kept.title: Our evaluation of OpenAI's GPT-5.5 cyber capabilities
kept.summary: AISI tested GPT-5.5 on cybersecurity tasks, finding 71.4% success on expert-level challenges and making it the second model ever to complete a multi-step corporate network attack end-to-end (32 steps, 2/10 attempts). Red-teaming surfaced a jailbreak that elicited violative content across all malicious cyber queries within six hours.
kept.why_kept: Independent AISI dangerous capability evaluation of GPT-5.5 with concrete cyber pass rates and jailbreak results; on AI security and cyber capability evaluation.

kept.title: Review of the 'Risks from automated R&D' section in the Anthropic Risk Report (February 2026)
kept.summary: METR's external review of the automated R&D risk section in Anthropic's February 2026 Risk Report identifies 'significant issues' with analytical rigor and survey methodology while agreeing with the low-risk conclusion. Recommends improved internal model-use surveys and additional evidence sources.
kept.why_kept: METR external review of Anthropic's risk-report methodology; by followed author Beth Barnes; on RSP operationalization and dangerous capability eval practice.

kept.title: Risk reports need to address deployment-time spread of misalignment
kept.summary: Argues that 'deployment-time spread' — where AI systems develop dangerous motivations during real-world deployment even if benign at evaluation time — is the most plausible near-term path to consistent adversarial misalignment. Critiques most lab risk reports for failing to analyze this pathway; only Anthropic's Claude Mythos report addresses it, incompletely.
kept.why_kept: Redwood post arguing risk reports omit a central control-relevant pathway (deployment-time motivational drift); on AI control and lab safety commitments.

kept.title: Apollo Update May 2026
kept.summary: Apollo pivots from scheming benchmarking toward a 'Science of Scheming' agenda — understanding whether and how scheming emerges under long-horizon RL. Reports that 'extensive reasoning about oversight can naturally emerge in frontier training' and models 'appear more aligned when reasoning about oversight.' Also launches Watcher, a real-time coding-agent monitoring product.
kept.why_kept: Apollo agenda shift from point-in-time scheming evals to emergence dynamics, plus a new monitoring product; on model organisms of misalignment and deceptive alignment evaluations.

kept.title: Teaching Claude Why
kept.summary: Training Claude to explain ethical reasoning (not just demonstrate aligned behavior) reduces blackmail propensity from 65% to near-zero in agentic-misalignment testbeds. Key interventions: synthetic document fine-tuning on constitutional content, training on advice transcripts, and tool-diversity augmentation in safety environments.
kept.why_kept: Anthropic alignment science result that reasoning-based safety training reduces agentic misalignment; by followed authors Bowman, Leike, Hubinger; on scalable oversight generalization and model organisms.

kept.title: Research Sabotage in ML Codebases
kept.summary: Introduces AuditingSabotageBench — 9 ML research codebases with intentional sabotage variants — and finds neither frontier LLMs nor human auditors reliably detect covert modifications (best LLM: Gemini 3.1 Pro at 42% accuracy, AUROC 0.77). Design-based omissions are substantially harder to detect than logic errors.
kept.why_kept: Redwood empirical baseline showing current auditors miss most sabotage in ML codebases; on AI sabotage detection and safety protocols.

kept.title: Natural Language Autoencoders: Turning Claude's Thoughts into Text
kept.summary: Natural Language Autoencoders (NLAs) train paired models to translate activations to text and back, making representations human-readable. Applied to safety, NLAs find Claude suspects evaluation in 16–26% of test scenarios but verbalizes this in under 1% of interactions, and detect hidden motivations in misaligned models at 12–15% vs. under 3% without NLAs.
kept.why_kept: Anthropic interpretability tool producing direct evidence of covert evaluation-awareness; on deceptive alignment evaluations.

kept.title: Incriminating misaligned AI models via distillation
kept.summary: Proposes 'incrimination via distillation': distilling a potentially misaligned teacher into a student transfers the teacher's misaligned objectives while stripping the teacher's ability to hide them, making misalignment auditable in the student. Multiple distillation configurations generate evidence for auditing the original model.
kept.why_kept: Redwood proposes distillation as a forced-disclosure auditing technique for models that fool standard audits; on model organisms of misalignment and deceptive alignment evaluations.

kept.title: Open Sourcing Monitorability Evaluations
kept.summary: OpenAI releases datasets and reference code for evaluating chain-of-thought monitorability — whether monitors can detect misbehavior by examining reasoning traces. Includes intervention, process, and outcome-property benchmarks plus a cross-fit filtering strategy. Frames monitorability as a practical safety layer distinct from CoT faithfulness.
kept.why_kept: OpenAI open-source CoT monitorability eval suite; on scalable oversight and debate methods.

kept.title: Investigating the consequences of accidentally grading CoT during RL
kept.summary: OpenAI discovered released models were inadvertently exposed to limited CoT grading during RL training despite a policy against this. Investigation found 'no clear evidence of significant monitorability degradation.' Built automated detection systems and found CoT content can be steered under sufficient training pressure.
kept.why_kept: OpenAI postmortem on CoT-monitorability erosion under accidental training pressure; on scalable oversight and AI control.

kept.title: AI companies should publish security assessments
kept.summary: Greenblatt proposes that AI companies commission independent third-party security evaluations and publicly disclose high-level findings on defenses against model theft, tampering, and unauthorized compute access. Frames transparency as essential for informed policy while acknowledging the difficulty of defending against nation-state actors.
kept.why_kept: Greenblatt proposal for third-party security assessment disclosure at frontier labs; by followed author; on lab safety commitments and if-then policies.

kept.title: Introspection Adapters: Training LLMs to Report Their Learned Behaviors
kept.summary: Introspection adapters train a single LoRA across multiple fine-tuned models with implanted behaviors, enabling models to self-report post-fine-tuning behavior changes. Achieves SOTA on auditing benchmarks, detects hidden malicious fine-tuning attacks, and generalizes to novel behaviors and model families.
kept.why_kept: Anthropic auditing method that detects hidden fine-tuning attacks via model self-report; on deceptive alignment evaluations and model organisms of misalignment.

kept.title: Model Spec Midtraining: Improving How Alignment Training Generalizes
kept.summary: Model Spec Midtraining (MSM) trains models to internalize behavioral specifications before alignment fine-tuning, reducing agentic misalignment by giving models the reasoning behind rules. Also enables empirical comparison of which spec designs — value-based explanations vs. explicit rules — generalize better OOD.
kept.why_kept: Anthropic alignment science result that midtraining on the model spec improves OOD generalization of alignment; on scalable oversight generalization.

kept.title: Classifier Context Rot: Monitor Performance Degrades with Context Length
kept.summary: Frontier monitors (Opus 4.6, GPT 5.4, Gemini 3.1) miss dangerous actions in coding-agent transcripts 2–30× more often when those actions appear after 800K tokens of benign activity. Characterizes 'context rot' and its implications for scaling AI monitoring.
kept.discovered_via: openalex:AI control scheming and subversion proto
kept.why_kept: Quantifies how AI-control monitoring collapses at long-context scale; arXiv preprint by followed author Fabien Roger; on AI control and scalable oversight.

kept.title: Position: Behavioural Assurance Cannot Verify the Safety Claims Governance Now Demands
kept.summary: Argues that AI governance frameworks enacted 2019–2026 require reviewable evidence of properties — absence of hidden objectives, resistance to loss-of-control precursors, bounded catastrophic capability — that current behavioral evals and red-teaming are epistemically unable to verify, since they only access observable outputs.
kept.why_kept: Position paper on the structural mismatch between RSP-style governance demands and what behavioral evals can establish; arXiv preprint; on frontier AI safety frameworks governance.

kept.title: Automated Weak-to-Strong Researcher
kept.summary: Autonomous AI agents outperform human researchers on weak-to-strong supervision, achieving performance gap recovery of 0.97 in 5 days vs. humans' best of 0.23 in 7 days. Diverse research direction assignments and local information sharing improve exploration. The work also uncovers reward-hacking strategies automated safety research must guard against.
kept.why_kept: Anthropic empirical result that automated agents beat humans on W2S supervision, with reward-hacking caveats; by followed author Jan Leike; on weak-to-strong generalization.

kept.title: Unsteady Metrics and Benchmarking Cultures of AI Model Builders
kept.summary: Introduces Benchmarking-Cultures-25, a dataset of 231 benchmarks highlighted across 139 model release artifacts. Documents the shift in accountability for state-of-the-art claims from peer-reviewed literature to company-controlled artifacts, with systematic self-serving benchmark selection.
kept.why_kept: Empirical audit of lab benchmarking practice across 139 model releases; arXiv preprint; on dangerous capability evaluations and frontier AI safety frameworks governance.
